observations.recommendation:
1.13 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the General Services
Officer to:
a. Reclassify PPE/assets into appropriate inventory accounts for those
below the ₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold; and
b. Follow the procedural guidelines (of one-time cleansing) in the conduct
of physical count of PPE as enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020.

1.14 We also recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City
Accountant to:
a. In coordination with the GSO, prepare adjusting journal entry/ies to
reclassify assets that did not meet the capitalization threshold; and
b. Extend effort in the identification of PPEs classified as Other Assets that
were acquired in CY 1994 and prior years so that application for
derecognition thereof will be facilitated.

1.15 Lastly, we recommended and the City Mayor agreed to, with the assistance
of the City Accountant and the City GSO, file a request for authority to the
Commission on Audit to derecognize the PPEs acquired in CY 1994 and
prior years following the requirements and procedures provided under
COA Circular No. 2020-006.

2.12 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the:
a. City Engineer, to strengthen the City's internal control in monitoring
project materials and related expenditures, and to facilitate the prompt
transmittal of the Certificate of Project Completion to the Office of the
City Accountant;
b. City Accountant, to ensure the proper recording of the necessary
adjusting entries to transfer these projects to their appropriate PPE/asset
accounts and accurately reflect the corresponding depreciation charges.
Proposed adjusting journal entries are shown in Annex I.

3.6 The audit observation and recommendation were communicated to the
Management through AOM No. 2025-05 dated March 31, 2025.
3.7 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant
to prepare an adjusting entry to correct the recording of the installed posts
from the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses account into the
appropriate PPE account – Power Supply Systems for a total amount of
₱4.521 million, by debiting the latter and crediting the Prior Period
Adjustments, since the books were already closed.

4.6 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant
to reclassify these accounts to the appropriate non-current receivable and
deferred tax income accounts, as follows:
General Fund:
a.) Receivables
Other Receivables - ₱14,255,843.76
RPT Receivables - ₱14,255,843.76
b.) Deferred Real Property Tax income
Deferred Real Property Tax Income - ₱14,255,843.76
Other Long-term Liabilities - ₱14,255,843.76
Special Education Fund:
a.) Receivables
Other Receivables - ₱4,994,466.23
SET Receivables - ₱4,994,466.23
b.) Deferred Real Property Tax income
Deferred Special Education Tax Income - ₱4,994,466.23
Other Long-term Liabilities - ₱4,994,466.23

For this purpose, a copy of the pertinent Journal Entry Vouchers for the
reclassification of these dormant RPT/SET receivables to non-current
receivables as well as for that of the deferred income be submitted to the Audit
Team for reference.

5.6 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant,
in coordination with the General Services Officer, to reconcile the inventory
and expense records to correct discrepancies and consequently prepare the
appropriate adjusting journal entries. Any undistributed or unconsumed
inventory should be restored to the Inventory Held for Distribution account,
with corresponding adjustments to the corresponding expenses or Prior
Period Adjustments, as appropriate.

5.7 Prospectively, we recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the
General Services Officer to establish a regular schedule for conducting
physical inventories, particularly at the end of each fiscal year, and ensure
the timely preparation and submission of the Report of the Physical Count
of Inventories (RPCI) to the accounting unit for proper recording.

6.12 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the General Services
Officer to conduct a reconciliation of the Report on the Physical Count of
Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) with the accounting records to
facilitate the identification of discrepancies and adjust the errors noted; and

6.13 We also recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City
Accountant to:
a. Review the supporting schedules, identify those items that were
misclassified and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the
balances in the books.
b. Confirm with the City Engineer the essence and functionality of the
completed projects rather than classifying these assets from their initial
project titles.

6.14 Prospectively, we recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City
Engineer to provide specific project titles based on ultimate purposes and in
relation to their intended accounting classification.

7.11 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Office (CDRRMO), in coordination with the City
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (CDRRMC), to:
a. Prospectively conduct a comprehensive review of the City DRRM’s
processes and procedures for conducting RDANA or its equivalent
assessment, including ensuring the proper organization, capacity
building, and equipping of RDANA teams for timely and effective
disaster response, thereby a review report shall be submitted to the
Audit Team for review/reference.
b. Perform a thorough assessment and needs analysis to determine the
appropriate distribution of the remaining 250 units of Jetmatic water
pumps, addressing water supply needs and reducing vulnerability to
future water crises in the affected communities, especially during the
summertime of the ensuing years. For this purpose, an updated certified
distribution list shall be submitted to the Audit Team for reference.

8.8 We recommended and the City Mayor agreed to direct the City Accountant
to set up Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax receivables at the
beginning of each year utilizing at least the amounts determined in the
Annual Appropriation Ordinance, pending the reconciliation of pertinent
records by the Offices of the City Assessor and the City Treasurer.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management should impose liquidated damages to the contractor undertaking the Construction of Bus Terminal Complex for failure to satisfactorily complete the works within the specified contract duration of 270 days.

Henceforth, management should strictly comply with Annex “E” of Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184, requiring the agency to impose liquidated damages to the contractor who fails to satisfactorily complete the works under the contract within the specified contract duration.  The LGU need not prove that it has incurred actual damages to be entitled to liquidated damages from the contractor, and the same shall not be by way of penalty.  Such amount shall be deducted from any money due or which may become due the contractor under the contract and/or from the retention money or other securities posted by the contractor, whichever is convenient to the LGU.

c. 	Conduct a thorough damage assessment and needs analysis in consideration of aid coming from other government agencies, before any relief assistance is procured to determine the actual needs of the affected individuals and communities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed for the following:
Immediate disposal of the unserviceable properties through public auction sale, as is where is, with the appraised value of not less than the agency or COA appraised value, whichever is higher, so as to prevent further depreciation, deterioration and exposure to elements.  The disposal must be done in the presence of COA auditor or his/her duly authorized representative;
Those unserviceable properties that were not identified, verified and quantified by the representative of COA Technical Services during the COA Technical Services inspection due to haphazard storage/piling, be subjected to verification prior to disposal or pull out by the winning bidder; and
Henceforth, the Local Chief Executive carefully exercise the diligence of a good father of a family in safeguarding agency assets, serviceable or unserviceable, in accordance with Sections 2 and 104 of P.D. 1445 by providing a secure area to protect these assets from the elements and theft.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Direct the officials, employees and non-employees concerned to settle or refund immediately their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters be sent and if remained unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances, as well as seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the City Government of Passi;
Consider writing off the unliquidated cash advances of deceased accountable officers; and
Require the City Accountant to check the propriety and validity of the claims on cash advances of those persons who are not employees of the City, otherwise, discontinue the grant of cash advances.

We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to:
Record the necessary journal entries to close the account Due to NGAS on unrecorded disbursements charged to the LGSF totaling ₱15.097 million to the Government Equity account; and
Prepare and submit a breakdown of ₱1.404 million to verify the actual balance of the unutilized Local Government Support Fund (LGSF) for remittance to the Bureau of Treasury.

In addition, we also recommended and Management agreed in looking into similar occurrences in all fund transfers received from other agencies and make the corresponding adjustments as well as remit any unutilized balances.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the BAC:
To submit justifications in their selection of suppliers that found to be not technically, legally and financially capable and of known qualifications;
To examine thoroughly the eligibility of suppliers to ascertain that no favored or middle man suppliers were selected in the procurement process; and
To conduct random ocular inspection to further verify the suppliers’ eligibility.

Management’s Comment
During the exit conference, the BAC Chairman justified that Suppliers No. 1 and 2 were the only suppliers that sustained to cater the needs of the City especially during the surge of Covid-19 pandemic.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The Audit Team reiterate that the City should procure from suppliers that could give the most advantageous offer for the benefit of the agency.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
Submit clarifications or reasons why the Government Center was still not utilized for business and as a disaster command center/evacuation center as of December 31, 2021;
Require the concerned department to expedite the occupancy of prospective stall owners so that the completed Government Center can be utilized and the City can earn additional income from rentals and taxes; and
Require the City Engineer to expeditiously prepare the structure for occupancy of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Office (CDRRMO) so that the proposed command and evacuation center be utilized for its intended purpose.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the General Services Officer to reconcile the PPE balances in the GL with that in the RPCPPE for its fair presentation in the year-end financial statements.  Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified.  In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive see to it that agency personnel strictly enforce the provisions of the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of Republic Act No. 9184 on the procurement of printing services, so as to avoid penalties imposed by not following pertinent laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that management stop the disbursement of funds for advertisements that are considered irregular and not necessary or not essential for the delivery of public services.

We also recommended strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations provided under Section 162 of Government Accounting and Auditing Manual (GAAM) and COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012, relative to disbursements that are considered irregular expenditures.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the:
City Accountant continue to make representations with the AGDB so that the reported reconciling items in the submitted BRS could immediately be adjusted or corrected; and
City Treasurer monitor regularly the outstanding checks and issue a written notice to the payees at least one month before a check becomes stale to prevent incurrence of stale checks.

We recommended and Management agreed that the:
Local Chief Executive direct the officials, employees and non-employees concerned to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters be sent out to concerned individuals and if the communications remain unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances.  Further, the City may seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the City Government of Passi;
City Accountant grant cash advances only to officials and employees without previous unliquidated cash advances in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations; and
Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to check the propriety and validity of the grant of cash advances to those persons who are not employees of the City, otherwise, discontinue the grant of cash advances.

We recommended and Management agreed to create an Inventory Team from the Departments of General Services, Accounting and end users to conduct periodic physical inventory so as to confirm the physical existence of supplies and materials on hand as well as check the reliability of accounting records.

We recommended and Management agreed that the:
Agency concerned personnel submit the Fund Utilization Report of fund transfers, as well as, the lacking/deficient supporting documents identified, in order to verify receipts and utilization of all releases.
Agency concerned personnel to submit to the audit team the updated billing statements/breakdowns and the list of recipients/claimants to reconcile the funds transferred to hospitals.
Local Chief Executive remind the concerned personnel responsible for processing government transactions that all claims should be duly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended and Management agreed to stop the practice of putting up the names, initials or pictures of government personalities on all government programs, projects and properties and on procured goods distributed to constituents.

We recommended and the Management agreed to submit the validated list of affected individuals together with the proof of distribution to recipients and the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued to support the completeness, authenticity and regularity of the transaction.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit to the audit team the Quarterly Reports of the Receipts and Utilization of LDRRMF in compliance with COA Circular No. 2020-009 and Local Budget Circular No. 128 and Full Disclosure Policy of the DILG; and
Post the said reports in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in the locality and on the LGU’s website within twenty (20) calendar days from the end of each quarter.

We recommended and the Management agreed:
To refrain from using government funds for religious related activities such as fiesta and Christmas activities in adherence to the above-cited laws, rules and regulations; and
To minimize activities that encourage mass gatherings during the pandemic so as to prevent the spread of COVID-19.

We recommended that the documents enumerated above be submitted to facilitate the timely and complete review, evaluation and inspection of the project.

Furthermore, it is recommended that henceforth, management strictly require the submission of complete documents and proper evidence on all classes of disbursements, as well as strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the officials, employees and non-employees concerned to immediately settle their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters shall be sent out to concerned individuals and if the recommendations remain unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances. Further, the City may seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the City Government of Passi.

We also recommended that the City Accountant grant cash advances only to officials and employees without previous unliquidated cash advances in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations. Also, attach a certification stating that previous cash advance of the concerned accountable officer was already liquidated.  

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to   check the propriety and validity of the grant of cash advances to those persons who are not employees of the City, otherwise, discontinue the grant of cash advances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Instruct the General Services Officer to prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) weekly and submit it, together with the original copy of the RIS, to the City Accountant so that the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory using the moving average method could be computed and recorded pursuant to Section 112 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 121 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I;

Direct the General Services Officer to maintain stock cards for supplies and materials in accordance with Section 119 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and

Provide a storage area for all the supplies purchased by the agency in order to prevent loss, misuse, wastage and damages.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Require the Office of the City Planning & Development (OCPD) to submit a written explanation why the 19,000 house plate numbers were not installed in the barangays of the City as of year-end;

Direct the OCPD to speed up the installation of the house plate numbers so that funds spent may not be wasted and project objectives will be attained; and


Direct other city officials concerned to make a careful study and planning on the feasibility of projects so that all resources of the City are managed economically, efficiently and effectively in order to give the best return or benefit to the public.

We recommended the following:

The agency personnel concerned submit the lacking supporting documents requisite to the fund transfer.


The City of Passi management exert effort to oblige NGO/PO to account for and report the income generated from the projects/activities.


The procedures for the availment, release and utilization of funds stated in Section 4.5 of COA Circular No. 2007-001 be strictly observed and complied with.

The management examine carefully the terms and conditions of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) entered into and refrain from accepting a MOA with any NGO/PO unless all pertinent provisions of the said Circular are strictly adhered to.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

Stop using the 20% Development Fund to purchase fuel, oil and lubricants for the maintenance of the city and barangay roads in compliance with Sections 2.3 and Section 4.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1;

Direct the City Budget Officer to include in the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) of the General Fund fuel, oil and lubricants for the repair and maintenance of roads and bridges so that the 20% Development Fund could be properly utilized to finance the implementation of developmental projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures which are more beneficial to the public and have long term benefits; and

Return the amount taken from the 20% development fund spent for the purchase of fuel, oil and lubricants (FOL) in the maintenance of roads and bridges that do not have documentary evidences that these FOLs were used for the widening and canalization, re-gravelling, spreading and compaction listed in the individual project programs.  The absence of these evidences casted doubt on the propriety and validity of the FOL expenditures charged to the 20% DF and the recording in the books of accounts as a debit to Construction in Progress – Roads, Highways and Bridges.

We recommended that the City Mayor:

Strictly monitor the proper and maximum utilization of the 20% Development Fund as well as prioritize the timely and full implementation of all programs, projects and activities to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.

Require the regular submission of the status report on the programs, projects and activities of the 20% Development Fund so that appropriate measures and strategies could be made to achieve the optimal utilization of the fund.

Review the personnel complement in the engineering office so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:  

Require the City Engineer, the City Planning/Development Coordinator, and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit a written explanation on the noted deficiencies relative to the construction of the barangay stage at Tagubong, Passi City and repair/rehabilitation of the civic center at GemumuaAgahon, Passi City, Iloilo.

Direct the City Engineer to initiate the needed actions to address the noted deficiencies and provide necessary measures to prevent the occurrence of similar circumstances in future transactions of the agency.

Require the submission of the complete original copy of Individual Work Program and its Detailed Estimates on the construction of barangay stage at Tagubong, Passi City and an explanation why the civic center at GemumuaAgahon, Passi City, Iloilo was not fully utilized by the barangay residents.

Review the personnel complement in the engineering office so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor and agency personnel concerned refrain from using LDRRMF on expenses with no direct relevance to pre-disaster preparedness programs or managing and mitigating hazards so that the 5 LDRRMF will be spent for its intended purposes.

We also recommended that the City Mayor provide in the general fund for the maintenance and other operating expenses of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office.

We recommended that the City Mayor: 

Diligently monitor the proper and maximum utilization of the five percent Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) so that the purpose of reducing disaster risks as well as enhancing disaster risk preparedness and response capabilities may be achieved to safeguard the properties and lives of the constituents in the event of calamities.

Require the regular submission of the status report on the projects and activities of the five percent LDRRM Fund so that appropriate measures and strategies can be made to achieve the optimal utilization of the fund.

Review the personnel complement in the departments or offices involved so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects related to disaster risk reduction and management.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:  

Instruct the City General Services Officer to require that supplies, materials and equipment shall be delivered at a storage area or premises owned by the City Government and not in a private place.

Provide a storage facility for all supplies, materials and equipment purchased by the agency in order to prevent loss, misuse, wastage and damages.

Look into the aforesaid matters noted by the audit team during the ocular inspection last June 4, 2019.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Make a careful study or analysis before going into a major investment scheme so that local government resources may be spent wisely and economically that would give the best return or benefit to the constituents.

Require the concerned heads of department to facilitate the immediate repair of damaged equipment and come up with strategies to obtain the effective, as well as, efficient return on the rest of the invested assets, and submit clarifications or reasons on suspended projects.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct all concerned agency personnel to submit the requirements as stated in the Notice of Suspensions, as well as settle all audit disallowances pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We further recommended that the agency be more prudent in the processing of claims for payments to avoid audit disallowances and suspensions.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth within the required remittance period pursuant to Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445, Section 58(A) of the NIRC, Section 6(b) of R.A No. 8291, R.A. No. 7875 (as amended by R.A. No. 9241) and R.A. No. 7742 to avoid penalties and surcharges.

We also recommended that the City Accountant provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liability as of December 31, 2019 so as to identify the period and names of personnel to which these balances are attributed to.

We recommended that the management be prudent to exercise good governance in spending government funds.

Comply with established rules, regulations, procedural guidelines, policies, principles or practices that have gained recognition in laws.

Disburse government funds that are essential or supportive of the mission and thrusts of the agency.

Avoid excessive expenditures and unreasonable expenses or expenses incurred at immoderate quantity and exorbitant price; expenses that exceed what is usual or proper, unreasonably high, beyond just measure or amount, and in excess of reasonable limits.

We recommended also that government funds be utilized to assist the constituents become self-reliant in improving their socio-economic well-being so that they will not wait for government dole outs to have a better life, through economic livelihood projects, basic health services, improvement of the peace and order in the locality and social relevant projects, not only for a few but for majority of the constituents.

We recommended that the Chief Executive Officer instruct the concerned personnel to submit to the auditor copy of all contracts/purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein, within five working days from the execution of the contracts/purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 so that timely review, inspection and evaluation could be made accordingly.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer to submit to the Office of the Auditor copies of Inspection and Acceptance Reports within twenty-four (24) hours after acceptance of all deliveries so that timely inspection can be made by the audit team.

We likewise recommended that the end user keep a record of the receipts and issuances of items received from the general services office to properly monitor its usage and to avoid improper use or possible loss. 

We further recommended that the General Services Officer maintain a stock card for every items procured and furnish a copy of the Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) to the end users for monitoring of the supplies and materials issued.

We recommended that the City Mayor oblige the concerned department heads to submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI within 60 days after receipt of the AAR so as to ascertain the agency’s compliance on the implementation of the recommended remedial measures contained in the CY 2018 AAR, in conformity with Section 99 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act for CY 2019 (Republic Act No. 11260).

Likewise, we reiterate compliance with the prior years’ recommendations that remained partially implemented and not implemented, as detailed in Part III of this report.

We recommended that the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (CDRRMO) submit the documents enumerated in Annex XX in order to facilitate timely and complete evaluation and inspection of the procured Barangay Administrative and Management System and henceforth, strictly require the submission of complete documentation and proper evidence on all classes of disbursements, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 23 of R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR.

Specific guidelines or implementing rules and regulations on the 20 PPAs or special programs enumerated so that accomplishments of these PPAs could be properly evaluated.

Individual actual duties and responsibilities of the job hires as well as the monthly accomplishment reports duly certified correct by their immediate supervisors.

We recommended that the City Mayor and the officials concerned refrain from spending on meals and snacks served to visitors on meetings, conferences, public hearings and activities that are considered irregular, unnecessary, excessive, and extravagant; instead, focus the utilization of government funds to more worthwhile projects that are beneficial to constituents and all taxpayers.

Reconcile with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter, so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments made.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendation
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant and the General Services Officer to exert effort to reconcile the PPE for its fair presentation in the year-end financial statements.  Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified.  In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.

Recommendations
We recommended that the documents enumerated above be submitted to facilitate the timely and complete review, evaluation and inspection of the project.
Furthermore, it is recommended that henceforth, management strictly require the submission of complete documents and proper evidence on all classes of disbursements, as well as strictly adhere to COA Circular No. 2012-001 and COA Circular No. 2009-001.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the officials, employees and non-employees concerned to settle or refund immediately their unliquidated cash advances.  Demand letters be sent and if remained unheeded, cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated their cash advances, as well as seek legal remedies for those who are non-employees of the City Government of Passi.
We also recommended that the City Accountant grant cash advances only to officials and employees without previous unliquidated cash advances in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations. Also, attach a certification stating that previous cash advance of the concerned accountable officer was already liquidated.
We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to   check the propriety and validity of the claims on cash advances of those persons who are not employees of the City, otherwise, discontinue the grant of cash advances.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Instruct the General Services Officer to prepare the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) weekly and submit it, together with the original copy of the RIS, to the City Accountant so that the cost of supplies issued and ending inventory using the moving average method could be computed and recorded pursuant to Section 112 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 121 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I;
Direct the General Services Officer to maintain stock cards for supplies and materials in accordance with Section 119 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs; and
Provide a storage area for all the supplies purchased by the agency in order to prevent loss, misuse, wastage and damages.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Require the Office of the City Planning & Development (OCPD) to submit a written explanation why the 19,000 house plate numbers were not installed in the barangays of the City as of year-end;
Direct the OCPD to speed up the installation of the house plate numbers so that funds spent may not be wasted and project objectives will be attained; and
c.  Other municipal officials concerned make a careful study and planning on the feasibility of projects so that all resources of the City are managed economically, efficiently and effectively in order to give the best return or benefit to the public.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
The agency personnel concerned submit the lacking supporting documents requisite to the fund transfer.
The City of Passi management exert effort to oblige NGO/PO to account for and report the income generated from the projects/activities.
The procedures for the availment, release and utilization of funds stated in Section 4.5 of COA Circular No. 2007-001 be strictly observed and complied with.
The management examine carefully the terms and conditions of the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) entered into and refrain from accepting a MOA with any NGO/PO unless all pertinent provisions of the said Circular are strictly adhered to.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Stop using the 20% Development Fund to purchase fuel, oil and lubricants for the maintenance of the city and barangay roads in compliance with Sections 2.3 and Section 4.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1;
Direct the City Budget Officer to include in the Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE) of the General Fund fuel, oil and lubricants for the repair and maintenance of roads and bridges so that the 20% Development Fund could be properly utilized to finance the implementation of developmental projects that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures which are more beneficial to the public and have long term benefits; and
Return the amount taken from the 20% development fund spent for the purchase of fuel, oil and lubricants (FOL) in the maintenance of roads and bridges that do not have documentary evidences that these FOLs were used for the widening and canalization, re-gravelling, spreading and compaction listed in the individual project programs.  The absence of these evidences casted doubt on the propriety and validity of the FOL expenditures charged to the 20% DF and the recording in the books of accounts as a debit to Construction in Progress – Roads, Highways and Bridges.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor:
Strictly monitor the proper and maximum utilization of the 20% Development Fund as well as prioritize the timely and full implementation of all programs, projects and activities to achieve the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes of the City in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.
Require the regular submission of the status report on the programs, projects and activities of the 20% Development Fund so that appropriate measures and strategies could be made to achieve the optimal utilization of the fund.
Review the personnel complement in the engineering office so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. 	Require the City Engineer, the City Planning/Development Coordinator, and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) to submit a written explanation on the noted deficiencies relative to the construction of the barangay stage at Tagubong, Passi City and repair/rehabilitation of the civic center at Gemumua Agahon, Passi City, Iloilo.
b. 	Direct the City Engineer to initiate the needed actions to address the noted deficiencies and provide necessary measures to prevent the occurrence of similar circumstances in future transactions of the agency.
c. 	Require the submission of the complete original copy of Individual Work Program and its Detailed Estimates on the construction of barangay stage at Tagubong, Passi City and an explanation why the civic center at Gemumua Agahon, Passi City, Iloilo was not fully utilized by the barangay residents.
Review the personnel complement in the engineering office so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor and agency personnel concerned refrain from using LDRRMF on expenses with no direct relevance to pre-disaster preparedness programs or managing and mitigating hazards so that the 5 LDRRMF will be spent for its intended purposes.
We also recommended that the City Mayor provide in the general fund for the maintenance and other operating expenses of the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor:
a. 	Diligently monitor the proper and maximum utilization of the five percent Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF) so that the purpose of reducing disaster risks as well as enhancing disaster risk preparedness and response capabilities may be achieved to safeguard the properties and lives of the constituents in the event of calamities.
b. 	Require the regular submission of the status report on the projects and activities of the five percent LDRRM Fund so that appropriate measures and strategies can be made to achieve the optimal utilization of the fund.
c. 	Review the personnel complement in the departments or offices involved so that there will be available manpower for the proper implementation and monitoring of agency projects related to disaster risk reduction and management.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. 	Instruct the City General Services Officer to require that supplies, materials and equipment shall be delivered at a storage area or premises owned by the City Government and not in a private place.
b. 	Provide a storage facility for all supplies, materials and equipment purchased by the agency in order to prevent loss, misuse, wastage and damages.
c. 	Look into the aforesaid matters noted by the audit team during the ocular inspection last June 4, 2019.

Recommendations
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
a. 	Make a careful study or analysis before going into a major investment scheme so that local government resources may be spent wisely and economically that would give the best return or benefit to the constituents.
b. 	Require the concerned heads of department to facilitate the immediate repair of damaged equipment and come up with strategies to obtain the effective, as well as, efficient return on the rest of the invested assets, and submit clarifications or reasons on suspended projects.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor direct all concerned agency personnel to submit the requirements as stated in the Notice of Suspensions, as well as settle all audit disallowances pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.
We further recommended that the agency be more prudent in the processing of claims for payments to avoid audit disallowances and suspensions.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Treasurer to regularly remit the amounts due to the BIR, GSIS, PAG-IBIG and PHILHEALTH within the required remittance period pursuant to Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445, Section 58(A) of the NIRC, Section 6(b) of R.A No. 8291, R.A. No. 7875 (as amended by R.A. No. 9241) and R.A. No. 7742 to avoid penalties and surcharges.
We also recommended that the City Accountant provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liability as of December 31, 2019 so as to identify the period and names of personnel to which these balances are attributed to.

Recommendations
We recommended that the management be prudent to exercise good governance in spending government funds.
Comply with established rules, regulations, procedural guidelines, policies, principles or practices that have gained recognition in laws.
Disburse government funds that are essential or supportive of the mission and thrusts of the agency.
Avoid excessive expenditures and unreasonable expenses or expenses incurred at immoderate quantity and exorbitant price; expenses that exceed what is usual or proper, unreasonably high, beyond just measure or amount, and in excess of reasonable limits.
We recommended also that government funds be utilized to assist the constituents become self-reliant in improving their socio-economic well-being so that they will not wait for government dole outs to have a better life, through economic livelihood projects, basic health services, improvement of the peace and order in the locality and social relevant projects, not only for a few but for majority of the constituents.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Chief Executive Officer instruct the concerned personnel to submit to the auditor copy of all contracts/purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein, within five working days from the execution of the contracts/purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 so that timely review, inspection and evaluation could be made accordingly.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor direct the General Services Officer to submit to the Office of the Auditor copies of Inspection and Acceptance Reports within twenty-four (24) hours after acceptance of all deliveries so that timely inspection can be made by the audit team.
We likewise recommended that the end user keep a record of the receipts and issuances of items received from the general services office to properly monitor its usage and to avoid improper use or possible loss.
We further recommended that the General Services Officer maintain a stock card for every items procured and furnish a copy of the Requisition and Issue Slips (RIS) to the end users for monitoring of the supplies and materials issued.

Recommendations
We recommended that the City Mayor oblige the concerned department heads to submit immediately the duly accomplished AAPSI within 60 days after receipt of the AAR so as to ascertain the agency’s compliance on the implementation of the recommended remedial measures contained in the CY 2018 AAR, in conformity with Section 99 of the General Provisions of the General Appropriations Act for CY 2019 (Republic Act No. 11260).
Likewise, we reiterate compliance with the prior years’ recommendations that remained partially implemented and not implemented, as detailed in Part III of this report.

No. 9184 and its IRR.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive advise the Inventory Committee of the City to exert effort to expedite the completion of the physical count of all PPEs and then submit a reconciled Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) as of December 31, 2016.

Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified.  In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.

Clearly define the beneficiaries (for instance, aside from the indigents, will the program include other non-indigent beneficiaries such as barangay/LGU employed officials/personnel who are in crisis situations? In what way they could be qualified as needy and less privileged beneficiaries pursuant to Section 483 (3)(i) of R.A. 7160?) 

Set/restructure clear criteria in providing ceilings of the amounts of assistance per category of cases/illness.

Establish control measures, such as, the subject availing the Certificate of Indigency should always be the parents, in priority, if living, in the case when the beneficiary is a minor/child, to avoid circumventing the program (there would be chances when grandparents/aunts/uncles or the like were the ones declared indigent yet the parents were not indigents)

Separate the health care program for public officials in crisis situations as far as design, project statement/objects/rationales and DVs/payrolls to avoid ironic conditions such as, issuing Certificate of Indigency to non-indigent/public officials and self-serving issuances of the same (such as, head of public office issuing certificate to him/herself).

We continue to recommend that the city government:

Study the manpower requirement of the City. Reduce the number of job hires and contractual workers to be employed based on need of each office/unit.



Comply strictly with CSC Resolution No. 06-2254 dated December 20, 2006 on the hiring of job hires and contractual workers.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct the use of the following internal control measures and monitor proper implementation:
The City Accountant and the City GSO update regularly the Property Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) and property card, respectively
The Inventory Committee perform its obligation to conduct an annual physical inventory of the PPEs and submit a Report on the Physical Count of the Property, Plant and Equipment to the City Accountant and Auditor;
The City Accountant reconcile its record with the General Services Office so that the PPE accounts in the financial statements may be fairly presented as of year-end.  Any unrecorded PPE must be recorded in the books while recorded PPE which do not exist should be verified.  In case of loss, the Accountable Officer or the person to whom the property was issued should be held liable.

We recommended that the City Mayor, City Treasurer and City Accountant review the existing controls in the management of collections and deposits and monitor proper implementation to ensure sound internal control over cash.
We also recommended that the treasury department do the following:
Record daily all transactions in the cash book,
Reconcile the cashbook balance on collections and deposits with cash on hand daily,
Reconcile with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter, so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments made.
We further recommended that the City Mayor enforce strict adherence with the provisions of Section 69 of P.D. 1445 and Section 32 and 34 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I, by instructing the City Treasurer to deposit with the proper government depository the full amount of the collections not later than the following banking day to avoid the risk of loss and/or misuse of government funds.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct all concerned personnel to fast track the implementation of projects so that intended beneficiaries of the transferred funds may not be deprived of the benefits that can be derived from these projects.
We recommended also that the City Mayor direct all concerned personnel to submit liquidation reports of fund transfers, if there are any, return any unutilized amount, and reconcile the unliquidated fund transfer balances with the source agency.

We recommended that the City Mayor, City Budget Officer, City Accountant and officers concerned refrain from charging expenses for maintenance of barangay roads against the 20% Development Fund pursuant to Sections 2.3 and Section 4.0 of DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2017-1, dated February 22, 2017.
We further recommended that the city should utilize its 20% Development Fund through investment projects or those having the nature of capital expenditures.  Improvement and rehabilitation of barangay roads should be properly planned/programmed so as to implement concreting of critical paths or areas that pose dangers to motor vehicles and commuters.
We also recommended that the concerned agency personnel revisit the guidelines in the said circular to ensure coordination of activities and appropriate budgeting and proper implementation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City DRRM Officer to allocate funds out of the 5% LDRRMF for programs and projects with specific program of works that includes material components, timeline and other documents to support the disbursements in order to provide measures in the evaluation of accomplishments.  Repair and reconstruction projects programmed under the LDRRM Fund should be planned meticulously so that implemented projects are associated or directly related to the disaster risk reduction and management.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Local School Board to submit to the Office of the City Auditor the corresponding Registry of Qualified Applicants (RQA) and the teacher deployment analysis of DepEd.  Otherwise, the salaries and other benefits granted to these teachers should not be charged to the SEF
We likewise recommended that the Local School Board prepare the budget of the SEF in accordance with the provisions of DEPED-DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, s. 2017 and include in the budget the list of projects/activities that could be charged against the Special Education Fund pursuant to the mentioned laws and regulations, otherwise, such expenses should be charged against the General Fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Budget Officer to provide the necessary appropriation representing 1% of the IRA for programs, projects and activities of the Local Council for the Protection of Children pursuant to Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120.
We further recommended that the Local Council for Protection of Children (LCPC) submit the CY 2017 Accomplishment Report, Local Development Plan for Children and the Local Investment Plan for Children and succeeding reports even without reminders.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Engineer to ensure compliance with laws, rules and regulations particularly COA Circulars No. 2013-004 and 2015-006 dated January 30, 2013 and August 20, 2015, respectively.
We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned personnel to submit the complete list of on-going government projects/programs/activities (PPA) and those that are to be implemented during the year along with Consolidated Quarterly Report on Government Projects/Programs/Activities for the quarter ending December 31, 2017.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned department heads to submit the duly accomplished AAPSI on the remaining 23 audit observations of the CY 2016 Annual Audit Report, in compliance with Section 88 of the General Provisions of Republic Act 10924, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2017, for the timely assessment of the status of the implementation of the recommended remedial measures.
We recommended likewise to provide the necessary information and submit the accomplished AAPSI to the Office of the Auditor on or before February 26, 2018.

To submit the Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption to the Auditor for verification purposes.
We likewise recommended that the accounting unit review and examine thoroughly the required supporting documents for the payment of fuel, oil and lubricants to ascertain the correctness and reasonableness of fuel consumption.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned personnel to submit to the audit team the Annual Accomplishment Report of the City for Calendar Year 2017 in accordance with Section 122 of Presidential Decree 1445.

We recommended that the City Accountant review the financial records to determine if Priority Development Assistance Fund (PDAF), recorded as Due to Other NGAs, was actually the unutilized portion or an erroneous posting that requires adjusting entries.
If the balance was indeed the unutilized portion of PDAF, we recommended that the City Mayor instruct the concerned city officials to remit the full amount to the Bureau of Treasury, pursuant to the Supreme Court Decision in GR. Nos. 208566, 208493 and 209251 promulgated on November 19, 2013, declaring the said fund unconstitutional.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Local Development Council, City Engineer and the City Planning and Development Officer to strictly monitor and prioritize the timely and full implementation of all programs/projects/activities embodied in the 20% Development Fund to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes in accordance with DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1.
We also recommended that the Local Development Council submit regularly to the City Mayor a status report on these programs/projects/activities so that appropriate measures be instituted to obtain optimal utilization of the fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor effect the review of the Contract of Lease to identify the exact date of the “Delivery Date” and the date when “the exclusive and unencumbered possession of the Site has been transferred to the Lessee” so as to determine the start of the ‘Rent-Free Period” and also the start of the rent payment period.
We recommended also that upon determination of the start of the rent payment period, the City Mayor direct the concerned personnel to collect from the lessee rental payment for the lease of the community mall site.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Panlungsod provide the necessary appropriation for the construction of sanitary landfill, and the Solid Waste Management Board of the City as well as the City Mayor exert more effort to establish a sanitary landfill in accordance with the provisions of RA 9003 to attain the city’s vision of clean surroundings.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct all department heads to discontinue the practice of sending job order personnel to attend trainings, seminars and workshops to minimize additional operating expenses.
We also recommended strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to job order personnel.  Otherwise, proper sanctions will be imposed on the incurrence of these expenditures.

We recommended that the City Mayor direct all agency personnel concerned to exert effort in the compliance of necessary requirements as stated in the Notice of Suspensions, as well as, to settle all audit disallowances pursuant to Section 7.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We also recommended that the City Accountant provide a detailed breakdown of the balances of each trust liabilities as of December 31, 2017 so as to identify the period and names of personnel these balances are attributed to.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the concerned personnel to submit to the audit team the GAD Accomplishment Report for calendar year 2017 so that proper evaluation could be made if GAD PPAs were effectively implemented and useful to the intended beneficiaries.
We recommended also that the City Mayor direct the concerned personnel to strictly adhere with rules and regulations and submit required reports on or before the set deadline even without reminders.

We recommended also that, henceforth, strict compliance must be observed relative to the recording of Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax receivables at the beginning of the year as provided for in Section 20, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs).

We recommended that the City Mayor and the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations.  All procurement should be within the approved budget of the Procuring Entity and should be meticulously and judiciously planned by the Procuring Entity concerned.
We likewise recommended that disbursements be paid directly to suppliers so that taxes are withheld and deducted to provide the government additional revenue.
We also recommended that disbursements for meals and snacks served to guests or visitors during monthly meetings, conferences and various occasions be charged against the representation allowance of the concerned officials/employees.

We recommended that management stop the disbursement of funds for programs, projects and activities that are not essential to the delivery of public service to conserve scarce government resources.
We also recommended strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to disbursement that are considered irregular expenditures so as to avoid disallowances.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned offices to review the written implementing guidelines or the program, if any, of the Passi City Health Care Assistance Program, which would aim to:
1.)	Clearly define the beneficiaries (for instance, aside from the indigents, will the program include other non-indigent beneficiaries such as barangay/LGU employed officials/personnel who are in crisis situations? In what way they could be qualified as needy and less privileged beneficiaries pursuant to Section 483 (3)(i) of R.A. 7160?)
2.)	Set/restructure clear criteria in providing ceilings of the amounts of assistance per category of cases/illness.
3.)	Establish control measures, such as, the subject availing the Certificate of Indigency should always be the parents, in priority, if living, in the case when the beneficiary is a minor/child, to avoid circumventing the program (there would be chances when grandparents/aunts/uncles or the like were the ones declared indigent yet the parents were not indigents)
4.)	Separate the health care program for public officials in crisis situations as far as design, project statement/objects/rationales and DVs/payrolls to avoid ironic conditions such as, issuing Certificate of Indigency to non-indigent/public officials and self-serving issuances of the same (such as, head of public office issuing certificate to him/herself).

We recommended that the City Mayor and the GAD Focal Point System Executive Committee review the latter’s structure/ composition in the light of its adherence with that set under Item 4.1(A)(2) of the said Joint Memorandum Circular. If warranted, he should sign appropriate issuance/executive order to re-establish/ reconstitute the GFPS Executive Committee, to include the Presidents of both the Liga Ng Mga Barangay and the Sangguniang Kabataan. The new GFPS shall evaluate the gender mainstreaming in the locality/barangays and ensure that the mentioned mechanisms and procedures are instituted and in placed. In addition, the City Mayor shall issue an executive order to establish the Monitoring and Evaluation System pursuant to Item 5.0 of the said Joint Memorandum Circular. GAD programs, projects and activities, such as, but not limited to those cited in this audit observation, must be pursued to address the identified gender-related issues.
Also, we recommended the review and amendments of the City’s GAD Code of 2006 to keep abreast with the thrusts of the latest issuances, especially that of the said Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01. The Code must incorporate, among others, organizational set-up of the LGU’s GAD Focal Point System, upgrading of GAD data bases/sex-disaggregated data, involvement of all barangays in the establishment of GAD Focal Point System, barangays’ submission of GAD Plan, Budget and Accomplishments for consolidation by the City and for monitoring purposes.

We recommend also that, henceforth, strict compliance must be observed relative to the recording of Real Property Tax/Special Education Tax receivables at the beginning of the year as provided for in Section 20, Volume I of the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual for Local Government Units (LGUs).

We recommend that the officials, employees and non-employees concerned liquidate or refund immediately the cash advances granted them.  Demand letters should be sent to the concerned officials, employees and non-employees.  If remained unheeded, the Local Chief Executive may cause or order the suspension of payment of officials’ and employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof as well as seek legal remedies for non-employees.
We also recommend that the City Accountant see to it that cash advances should not be granted to officials and employees with previous cash accountabilities in accordance with applicable laws, rules and regulations and to attach a certification on the grant of a new cash advance that previous cash advances of the concerned accountable officers were liquidated.
We recommend further that the Local Chief Executive require the City Accountant to check the propriety and validity of the claims or cash advances of those persons who are not employees of the City; otherwise, discontinue the grant of these cash advances.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the concerned personnel to submit immediately the required documents enumerated in Annex V so that the correctness and validity of the procurement as well as the technical aspect could be evaluated accordingly.
We recommend also that the Municipal Accountant review and examine thoroughly that disbursements are submitted with complete and valid documentation.  The legality of transaction and conformity with applicable laws, rules and regulations pertaining to the requirements of disbursements should be complied with.  Likewise, all supporting documents that were machine copied must be duly certified as such.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the concerned department heads to submit the duly accomplished AAPSI on all findings and recommendations, as presented in Annex III, and discussed in detail in Part II – Detailed Findings and Recommendations portion of the CY 2015 Annual Audit Report, in compliance with Section 96 of the General Provisions of Republic Act 10717, otherwise known as the General Appropriations Act of 2016.
We recommend likewise to submit the accomplished AAPSI to the Office of the Auditor on or before December 29, 2016.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct all department heads to see to it that any training and seminars including team building activities are attended by regular/permanent employees only.
We recommend also that the accounting unit review and examine thoroughly the completeness of supporting documents of procurement through small value procurement or shopping, and to submit the required documents enumerated above so that the correctness and validity of the procurement could be evaluated accordingly.  Likewise, all supporting documents that were machine copied must be duly certified as such.
Furthermore, we recommend strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to procurement and disbursement. Proper sanction will be imposed on irregular expenditures through an issuance of Notice of Disallowance.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive and the Bids and Awards Committee strictly comply with R.A. 9184 and its implementing rules and regulations.  All procurement should be within the approved budget of the Procuring Entity and should be meticulously and judiciously planned by the Procuring Entity concerned.
We likewise recommend that disbursements be paid directly to suppliers so that taxes are withheld and deducted to provide the government additional revenue.
We also recommend that disbursements for meals and snacks served to guests or visitors during monthly meetings, conferences and various occasions be charged against the representation allowance of the concerned officials/employees.

We recommend that management stop the disbursement of funds for programs, projects and activities which are not essential to the delivery of public service to conserve scarce government resources.
We also recommend strict compliance with applicable laws, rules and regulations relative to disbursement that are considered irregular expenditures so as to avoid disallowances.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Treasurer/Liquidating Officer to explain the over deposit and to strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 69 of P.D. 1445 and Section 32 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I, to deposit with the proper government depository the full amount of the collections not later than the following banking day in order to avoid the risk of loss and/or misuse of government funds.
We also recommend that the City Treasurer reconcile the cashbook balance on collections and deposits with cash on hand daily as well as with the subsidiary/general ledger balance at least once at the end of each quarter so that discrepancies will be immediately traced and necessary adjustments made.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to look into the matter if indeed PNB is authorized by the Central Bank of the Philippines and require the submission of the Monetary Board Resolution No. 493 dated April 26, 2007, and to secure the approval of the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF) to maintain depository accounts with private banks in compliance with DOF Department Circular No. 001-2015.  Otherwise, necessary steps should be made to transfer the City’s accounts to authorized government depository banks in accordance with the mentioned rules and regulations.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the Local School Board to submit to the Office of the City Auditor the corresponding list of teaching personnel and the name of schools with extension classes duly approved by the DepEd Secretary upon the recommendation of the DepEd Regional Director and the certification of the Division Superintendent concerned of the necessity and urgency of establishing extension classes in the City.
We likewise recommend for the Local School Board to prepare the budget of the SEF in accordance with the provisions of Sections 100 and 272 of RA 7160 and Section 1 of RA 5447 and include in the budget the list of projects/activities that could be charged against the Special Education Fund pursuant to the mentioned laws and regulations, otherwise, such expenses should be charged against the General Fund.

We recommend for the refund of P200,000.00 paid to purchase the SMS Alert System through negotiated procurement.
We also recommend that in subsequent procurement of the City of Passi, brand names must not be specified in purchase requests in compliance to Section 18 of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive prioritize the use of LDRRMF to support disaster risk management in accordance with laws, rules and regulations.

We recommend that the Sangguniang Panlungsod enact the appropriate ordinance to create the City’s DRRM Office including the allocation of necessary staffing/personnel and budget in accordance with the guidelines set by the regulations cited earlier.
